details.text: Derivative: أَصَابَ means to afflict and carries the meaning of hitting the target or mark. As such, the مُّصِيبَةٍ (calamity or the thing that strikes you) which is derived from أَصَابَ refers to something that was targeted to befall upon or afflict someone.

Derivative: تَغْفِرُوا from غَفَرَ which means to forgive. In origin غَفَرَ (Ghafara) means to cover and conceal something. The meaning here can be to cover for your spouse i.e., offering a protection for their mistakes.
Derivative: تَصْفَحُوا۟ – from صَفَحَ and it means to overlook. صَفْحَة (Safhah) means a page and صَفَحَ means to turn a page i.e., overlook what has happened and move on.

Derivative: فِتْنَةٌ (Fitnah) means a trial and test. It is from فَتَنَ which in Arabic can refer to the process of smelting gold in fire to test its quality. فِتْنَةٌ (Fitnah) here refers to an extremely difficult test.
Derivative: يُوقَ means to be protected. It is from وَقِيَ – يَقِي – وِقايَة which means to protect
Derivative: شُحَّ refers to avarice, greed and stinginess i.e., one’s obsessive concern for oneself above everything.
Derivative: شَكُورٌ is an attribute which with a person means “Grateful”, however, with Allah its meaning is closer to the meaning of “Appreciative”.
Derivative: حَلِيمٌ means to be forbearing and compassionate.
Derivative: صَوَّرَ – تَصْوِير means to mould, fashion and form.
Derivative: بَشَرٌ (Bashar) “A human (mortal) being” has origins in the word بِشْر (Bishr - skin) because human beings reveal more skin than other creatures.

- “I swear by my Rabb (Lord – Master)”. With oaths, the object is a proof of the subject. The object of the oath here is Allah Himself (رَبِّي – My Master). On account of Him being My master is enough proof for the subject i.e., that He will raise you after death.

Derivative: التَّغَابُنِ (At-Taghaabun) can be understood to refer to the ultimate winning and losing. Nobody will win like that day’s winner, and nobody will lose like that day’s loser. The day of At-Taghaabun (يَوْمُ التَّغَابُنِ) will be a day of great winning and a day of great losing. The Mufassireen have explained التَّغَابُنِ (At-Taghaabun) to refer to the day that the believers will be given what the disbelievers were deprived of, in terms of Jannah.
